chunks.text: According to Shah and Burke (2005) and Richardson (2007), a labour shortage occurs when there is insufficient supply of workers willing to work under current market conditions, particularly prevailing wages. Factors causing shortages include lags in wage adjustments, labour supply responsiveness, and insufficient labour market information. Thus, methodologies for identifying shortages should consider factors like employment, wages, working hours, and vacancies. The top-down methodology utilised multiple data sources, including quantity indicators (employment levels, vacancies) and price indicators (earnings, wage premiums), applying specific thresholds to identify shortages. It then adopted a "traffic light" approach, whereby the final determination of a shortage was made by considering evidence from a certain percentage of indicators, rather than relying on evidence from a single indicator. 25 26

Using a benchmark period to set threshold values reduces the risk of incorrect inclusion and provides three (3) additional benefits (MAC 2010): • First, as the economy evolves, the indicators of labour market shortages also change. This means that fewer occupations are identified as being in shortage during periods of weak economic growth. • Second, indicators change along with skill shortages rather than other types of shortages that are more stable over time. • Finally, it eliminates the requirement to reevaluate the threshold values for each assessment period. This means that the suitability of using a p50+50% or p75 threshold need only be assessed for the benchmark period. MyCOL 2024/2025 used 2022 as the benchmark period. Figure 3 shows that 2022 recorded the highest annual gross domestic product (GDP) growth (8.7%) with an unemployment rate of 3.9% as compared to 2011 and 2022. The economic growth was higher in 2022 than in 2014. This qualifies 2022 to be selected as the benchmark period for this analysis. 31 32

The Regional Corridor Development Authority (RECODA) faces key challenges in Sarawak, including manpower shortages due to high industry demand and a smaller working-age population compared to Peninsular Malaysia, as well as a skills deficiency in Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) and STEM skills. Worker retention is also a concern, as many leave for higher-paying jobs. Although RECODA promotes TVET and short courses to equip workers with real-world skills, implementation is hindered by limited incentives. To address these issues, RECODA supports human capital development under the Sarawak Corridor of Renewable Energy (SCORE), focusing on job training, community empowerment, and infrastructure to boost local livelihoods. Under RECODA, several programmes target the B40 group in Sarawak to improve employability and skills across various industries. In Agriculture, courses such as Fresh Bunch Fruit Harvesting and Collection Management and Plantation Worker Skills Courses provide essential training in agriculture and plantation management. Industrial skills programmes include the Blaster & Painter Level 1 (PCT), Wireman Second Grade, and Oil & Gas Construction Crew training, focusing on technical skills for specific industries. Service industry skills training, like Frontline Customer Service, enhances communication and customer handling abilities. Additionally, the Communication and Digital Marketing Upskilling programme focuses on enhancing digital and marketing skills, providing participants with cutting-edge tools for social media and audience engagement. These programmes aim to boost employability for the B40 group across sectors such as agriculture, industry, services, and digital communication. 65 66

The programme is conducted by Sarawak Skills. • Plantation Worker Skills Courses: Skills development in planting, crop care, pest control, and garden maintenance. The training is provided by Centre for Technology Excellence Sarawak (CENTEXS). Service Industry Skills • Frontline Customer Service: This programme provides intensive training in frontline customer service skills, including effective communication, complaint handling, and enhancing customer experiences. Launched in 2022, the course is conducted by University of Technology Sarawak (UTS). Industrial Skills and Technical Training • Blaster & Painter Level 1 (PCT): Basic techniques in blasting and painting. Sarawak Skills manages the course. • Wireman Second Grade: Fundamentals of electrical wiring system installation and maintenance facilitated by the University of Technology Sarawak (UTS). • Oil & Gas Construction Crew: Specialised training in the construction aspects of the oil and gas industry offered by the CENTEXS. Note on TOP-DOWN DATA INDICATORS: The indicators are identified by looking for data that have occupational information which could point to evidence of shortage. A threshold is then set to determine the matrix of what would be considered in shortage or not. Indicators Shortage Rationale 1. Working hours growth (1 year) An increase in the median number of hours worked per week may suggest that rising labour demand is being met through longer working hours among the existing workforce. 2. Working hours growth (3 years) 3. Employment growth (1 year) An increase in the number of employees is often indicative of increasing demand for that occupation. 4. Employment growth (3 years) 5. Wage premium growth (1 year) An increase in wages often reflects rising demand for labour in that occupation. 6. Wage premium growth (3 years) 7. Education level decrease (1 year) Lowering education requirements can signal labour shortages, as employers adjust hiring criteria to secure workers for hard-to-fill positions. 8. Education level decrease (3 years) 9. Number of vacancies A high number of vacancies typically indicate increasing demand for labour that exceeds current supply. This indicator follows the same rationale as the vacancy measure, but it 10. Vacancy rate (% of employment) accounts for employment size within each occupation. Shifts in the education level can indicate employer adjustments in hiring 11. Falling education requirements in job postings standards to address hard-to-fill vacancies. 12. Degree of persistence in occupational shortage Repeated listing in the MyCOL over successive years suggests that it has been persistently difficult to fill. This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Chief Executive Officer, Chief Operating Officer, Executive Director, Company Director, Managing Director, Enterprise Director, Managing-Director of Organisation, Manager, General Manager, Operations Manager, Director, Administrator, Technical Manager, Head of Operation, Assistant Manager, and Technical Director. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are across all experience levels, from entry to senior level, but predominantly at the senior level, equivalent to more than 5 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for the majority of vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in business and management, engineering technology, and personal skills. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are planning and organising, problem solving, and initiative and innovation. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in business operations and financial activities skills, operating procedures and processes skills, and communication and collaboration skills. The top technology tools nominated were business intelligence and decision support software, customer relationship management (CRM) software, and project management software. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. Around 40% to half of the firms claimed that this occupation is neither more nor less hard-to-fill as compared to a year ago. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between 3-4 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are applicants lack relevant job experience, too few applicants for the vacancies, and applicants lack the necessary credentials or qualifications. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to raise wages, expand local recruitment efforts, and increase employees’

The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are applicants lack relevant job experience, too few applicants for the vacancies, and applicants lack the necessary credentials or qualifications. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to raise wages, expand local recruitment efforts, and increase employees’ training. v. In the horticulture sector, stakeholders expressed significant challenges in finding candidates with strong leadership skills. They highlighted the difficulty in recruiting individuals capable of managing diverse teams, driving strategic initiatives, and effectively navigating complex regulatory environments. 105 106

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Finance Manager, Account Manager, Audit Manager, Finance Director, Chief Financial Controller, Risk Manager, Head of Procurement, Procurement Manager, Risk Modelling Manager, and Actuarial Valuation Manager. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are across all experience levels, from entry to senior level, but predominantly at the senior level, equivalent to more than 5 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for the majority of the vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in business and management, literacy and numeracy, and generic programmes and qualifications not further defined. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are numeracy, planning and organising, and problem solving. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in business operations and financial activities skills, safety and hazard management skills, and human resources skills. The top technology tools nominated were accounting software, financial analysis software, and enterprise resource planning ERP software. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. Around 40% to half of the firms claimed that this occupation is harder to fill as compared to a year ago. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between more than 6 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are too few applicants for the vacancies, applicants lack relevant job experience, and expected compensation is beyond the market rate. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to expand local recruitment efforts, raise wages, and increase employees’ training. v. Stakeholders from the insurance and banking sectors

is hard to fill are too few applicants for the vacancies, applicants lack relevant job experience, and expected compensation is beyond the market rate. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to expand local recruitment efforts, raise wages, and increase employees’ training. v. Stakeholders from the insurance and banking sectors highlighted significant challenges in recruiting and retaining finance managers and other key roles. In the insurance industry, stiff competition from countries like Singapore and the UK has made it increasingly difficult to retain trained actuaries, with consulting firms offering highly attractive packages that further drain the talent pool. Additionally, there is a limited supply of candidates with relevant industry experience and expertise in the local market. In the banking sector, both finance and risk management were identified as niche skill sets that are in high demand but difficult to find, exacerbating the sector’s struggle to fill these specialised positions. 109 110

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Chief Human Resources Officer, Human Resource Manager, Training Manager, Occupational Safety and Health Manager, Occupational Safety Manager, Health, Safety and Environment, and Quality Manager. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are ranging from junior to senior level, but predominantly at the junior level, equivalent to 1-3 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for the majority of vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in business and management, generic programmes and qualifications not further defined, and basic programmes and qualifications. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are planning and organising, oral communication, and initiative and innovation. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in safety and hazard management skills, communication and collaboration skills, and environmental management skills. The top technology tools nominated were audio/video conferencing software, human resources software, and database management software. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. Around 40% to half of the firms claimed that this occupation is neither more nor less hard-to-fill as compared to a year ago. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between more than 6 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are applicants lack the necessary credentials or qualifications and relevant job experience, while post-pandemic cost-cutting prevents firms from offering market- rate salaries. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to increase employees’ training, raise wages, and expand local recruitment efforts. v. Stakeholders from the horticulture sector expressed concerns about attracting qualified

applicants lack the necessary credentials or qualifications and relevant job experience, while post-pandemic cost-cutting prevents firms from offering market- rate salaries. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to increase employees’ training, raise wages, and expand local recruitment efforts. v. Stakeholders from the horticulture sector expressed concerns about attracting qualified human resource managers, noting that the geographic location of many horticultural operations can be a significant barrier. Potential candidates often prefer roles in urban centres, making it challenging to attract talent to more rural or remote areas where horticulture businesses are typically based. This geographic factor limits the pool of available candidates for key HR roles in the sector. 113 114

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Strategic Planning Manager, Corporate Planning Manager, Programme Manager, and Regulatory Affairs Manager. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are across all experience levels, from entry to senior level, but predominantly at the entry level, equivalent to fresh graduate. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for the majority of vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in education, business and management, and mathematics and statistics. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are planning and organising, numeracy, and initiative and innovation. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in data, analytics, and databases skills and business operations and financial activities skills. The top technology tools nominated were cost modelling software, human resources software, and project management software. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. In addition, majority of the firms mentioned that this occupation is neither more nor less hard-to-fill as compared to a year ago. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between more than 6 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are too few applicants for the vacancies, applicants lack the required technical skills, and relevant job experience. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to expand local recruitment efforts and expand international recruitment efforts. v. Stakeholders from the life sciences sector raised concerns about the lack of specialised education for policy and planning managers, particularly in regulatory affairs. Currently, no major directly focuses on regulatory affairs, leaving the industry to rely on employees

looking to expand local recruitment efforts and expand international recruitment efforts. v. Stakeholders from the life sciences sector raised concerns about the lack of specialised education for policy and planning managers, particularly in regulatory affairs. Currently, no major directly focuses on regulatory affairs, leaving the industry to rely on employees with backgrounds in pharmacology and pharmacy. These professionals often have knowledge of the Medical Devices Directive (MDD) and Medical Device Regulation (MDR) systems, but there remains a gap in formal training specific to the regulatory frameworks required for the sector. 117 118

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Production Manager, Production Planner, Production Controller, Business Development Manager, Compliance Manager, Supply Chain Manager, Business and Product Development Manager, Sustainability Manager, Project Manager, and Assistant Project Manager. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are across all experience levels, from entry to senior level, but predominantly at the senior level, equivalent to more than 5 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for the majority of vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in business and management, engineering, manufacturing and construction not further defined, and manufacturing and processing. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are planning and organising, initiative and innovation, and oral communication. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in communication and collaboration skills, business operations and financial activities skills, and operating procedures and processes skills. The top technology tools nominated were project management software, computer aided manufacturing, and industrial control and automation software. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. In addition, majority of the firms mentioned that this occupation is neither more nor less hard-to-fill as compared to a year ago. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between 5-6 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are applicants lack relevant job experience, too few applicants for the vacancies, and applicants lack the required technical skills. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to expand local recruitment efforts, increase employees’ training, and raise wages. v. Stakeholders from the insurance

main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are applicants lack relevant job experience, too few applicants for the vacancies, and applicants lack the required technical skills. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to expand local recruitment efforts, increase employees’ training, and raise wages. v. Stakeholders from the insurance and banking sectors highlighted significant challenges in filling business services manager roles, particularly in product development within the life insurance industry. These positions are highly specialised, especially for candidates with a comprehensive understanding of distribution channels, and the demand far exceeds the available talent pool. In the insurance sector, attracting younger talent is becoming increasingly difficult, as millennials and Generation Z often prefer career paths that offer more flexibility and stability over traditional sales roles. At the leadership level, many skilled professionals are seeking overseas or regional roles to gain broader exposure and benefit from more rewarding compensation packages. The fast-paced nature of the role, driven by evolving technology and societal trends, requires incumbents to stay continuously updated and creatively adapt to close deals. However, the sales profession remains stigmatised, with many perceiving it as pushy and unprofessional, further deterring top talent. Additionally, the pool of candidates with specialised financial, insurance, or Syariah expertise is limited. In the banking sector, niche skill sets remain scarce, with too few applicants available to meet demand for key roles, such as finance and risk management. 121 122

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Quality Control Manager and Quality Assurance Manager. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are predominantly at the senior level, equivalent to more than 5 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for all vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in manufacturing and processing, biological and related sciences, and engineering, manufacturing and construction not further defined. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are planning and organising, initiative and innovation, and problem solving. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in business operations and financial activities skills, production processes and machinery skills, and communication and collaboration skills. The top technology tools nominated were quantitative schedule and risk analysis software, regulatory compliance software, and computer aided manufacturing. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. In addition, majority of the firms mentioned that this occupation is neither more nor less hard-to-fill as compared to a year ago. The average time taken to fill these vacancies ranges between more than 6 months. The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are applicants lack relevant job experience, too few applicants for the vacancies, and applicants lack other required skills. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to raise wages, and establish or expand partnerships with education or training providers focused on recruitment of graduates. v. these challenges, firms are looking to raise wages, and establish or expand partnerships with education or training providers focused on recruitment of graduates. v. The Malaysian Association of Clinical Biochemists (MACB) emphasised the critical role of Quality Managers in advancing the field of clinical biochemistry. These professionals are essential for upholding high standards, ensuring quality control, and maintaining public trust in clinical practices. However, finding qualified individuals who meet the necessary criteria remains a challenge. Quality Managers are key to MACB’s mission of promoting excellence within the industry, making their expertise vital for the sector's continued success and reliability. 125 126

The main reasons the occupation is hard to fill are too few applicants for the vacancies, applicants lack other required skills, and relevant job experience. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to expand local recruitment efforts, raise wages, and convince workers to delay retirement. v. This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Plantation Manager, Horticulture Manager, Agricultural Production and Operation Manager, Agricultural Manager, Livestock Manager, and Forestry Production and Operation Manager. ii. The majority of firms reported that vacancies within this occupation group are predominantly at the senior level, equivalent to more than 5 years of working experience. A minimum of Bachelor’s Degree qualification is required for the majority of vacancies listed by the respondents in the CfE survey. Priority is given to those with backgrounds in agriculture, biological and related sciences, and personal skills. iii. The top basic skills needed for this occupation group are initiative and innovation, planning and organising, and teamwork. In terms of specific skills, the top requirements are expertise in human resources skills, business operations and financial activities skills, and communication and collaboration skills. The top technology tools nominated were database management software, automated vehicle guidances, and content workflow software. Based on the input from the CfE survey, majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. In addition, majority of the firms mentioned that this occupation is neither more nor less hard-to-fill as compared to a year ago. To overcome these challenges, firms are looking to raise wages, expand local recruitment efforts, and outsource this job function. v. In the timber sector, training workers to

overcome these challenges, firms are looking to raise wages, expand local recruitment efforts, and outsource this job function. v. Stakeholders from the timber, horticulture, agribusiness, and livestock sectors raised significant concerns about the challenges in filling agricultural, forestry, and livestock production manager roles. In the timber sector, training workers to become plantation managers takes a considerable amount of time, with most candidates lacking the necessary technical skills. In horticulture, the role requires a unique combination of expertise in both environmental sustainability and business management, making it difficult to find candidates with the right skills and experience. Similarly, in agribusiness, agrofood processing, and livestock, there is a noticeable shortage of local candidates with the necessary skills. These positions require extensive hands-on experience in farm operations, which is often difficult to find in the domestic workforce. 133 134

The Malaysia Logistic Supply Chain Association (MLSCA) and the Federation of Malaysian Freight Forwarders (FMFF) stressed the vital role of Supply, Distribution, and Related Managers in ensuring seamless supply chain operations. These professionals are tasked with meeting customer expectations, managing costs effectively, and supporting global trade and sustainability initiatives. This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Mathematician (Applied Mathematics), Actuary, Pricing Actuarie, Actuarial Executive, and Chief Actuary. ii. Stakeholders from the insurance sector highlighted several key challenges in recruiting mathematicians, actuaries, and statisticians, particularly in relation to highly specialised roles such as those required for actuarial science and International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS 17) positions. The market is characterised by a limited talent pool, with scarce skills and intense competition for qualified candidates. Actuarial roles demand a unique blend of expertise in mathematics, statistics, and finance, and the rigorous qualification process, which includes passing multiple exams from professional bodies like the Society of Actuaries (SOA) or the Institute and Faculty of Actuaries (IFoA), can deter potential candidates or delay their entry into the workforce. Furthermore, the growing importance of risk management and data-driven decision-making has increased demand for actuaries not only within the insurance industry but also in consulting, finance, and other sectors, leading to greater competition for talent. Local expertise is also in short supply, with Malaysia competing against regional markets like Singapore and Hong Kong for the same pool of talent, particularly in reinsurance. These factors, combined with the scarcity of experienced professionals, have made it increasingly difficult to find candidates with the necessary skills, technical knowledge, and experience to fill these critical roles. iii. Based on the input from the consultation, the banking sector primarily seeks mathematicians, actuaries, and statisticians with at least 3-5 years of experience for most roles, though demand increases for more senior positions requiring 8 years or more. Leadership roles, especially in higher-tier positions, typically call for 10 to 15 years of experience, reflecting the need for deep expertise and proven strategic capabilities in the field. iv. To address the shortage of

experience for most roles, though demand increases for more senior positions requiring 8 years or more. Leadership roles, especially in higher-tier positions, typically call for 10 to 15 years of experience, reflecting the need for deep expertise and proven strategic capabilities in the field. iv. To address the shortage of mathematicians, actuaries, and statisticians in the banking sector, several measures have been implemented. These include encouraging professionals to continue pursuing and completing their actuarial qualifications through examination support and benefits. Industry workshops are organised to promote knowledge sharing and ensure that professionals stay informed about market trends. Fresh graduates are also recruited and groomed for future roles, with a focus on developing talent from within the organisation. Additionally, initiatives such as clear career development paths, education assistance programmes, actuarial study support, and attractive salary and benefits packages are in place to motivate and retain talent. v. The Actuarial Society of Malaysia (ASM) highlighted a significant shortage of experienced actuaries, driven by the rigorous nature of actuarial exams and the requirement for extensive on-the-job experience. This talent gap is further aggravated by the competition from other Asian regions, which are actively attracting skilled professionals. Actuaries are essential for managing complex financial risks, particularly in the expanding insurance and takaful sectors, as well as meeting the increasing demand for pension and retirement planning solutions. Addressing this shortage is critical to supporting the industry's growth and stability. 153 154

majority of the firms are unlikely to automate the tasks of this occupation in the coming year. iv. Stakeholders from the timber, petrochemical, machinery, and electronics sectors raised significant concerns regarding the recruitment of mechanical engineers. In the timber manufacturing sector, finding qualified candidates is challenging, and even when they are available, many are deterred by the long working hours and demanding work environments. Additionally, candidates are increasingly demanding higher wages due to rising inflation and the weakening Ringgit. The petrochemical industry also faces a shortage of candidates with essential skills in maintenance, troubleshooting, and safety. In the machinery and equipment sector, young engineers tend to favour working with software over hands-on plant work, and many are drawn to higher-paying roles in semiconductor manufacturing. Competing with multinational corporations (MNCs) in terms of pay remains a significant challenge. The electrical and electronics (E&E) sector reports that many candidates do not meet the necessary skill requirements, while Generation Z workers are increasingly prioritising work-life balance. Similarly, the life sciences sector struggles to find talent with experience in medical devices, further contributing to the skills gap across these industries. vi. Malaysia’s aerospace, pharmaceutical, and medical device sectors are already key contributors to the nation’s industrial landscape, each attracting major global players and

increasingly prioritising work-life balance. Similarly, the life sciences sector struggles to find talent with experience in medical devices, further contributing to the skills gap across these industries. vi. Malaysia’s aerospace, pharmaceutical, and medical device sectors are already key contributors to the nation’s industrial landscape, each attracting major global players and supporting significant technological advancement. The aerospace sector, home to companies manufacturing critical components such as wings, avionics, and drones, is set for substantial growth, with revenues projected to reach RM55 billion by 2030. Similarly, the pharmaceutical and medical device industries play an essential role in healthcare, driven by rising demand for innovative products like minimally invasive medical technologies and biologics. Both sectors are expected to see marked expansion, supporting Malaysia’s ambitions for a high- value, innovation-driven economy. The NIMP 2030 prioritises high value-added opportunities in aerospace, pharmaceutical, and medical device sectors, recognising their potential to drive Malaysia’s industrial and economic growth. In aerospace, the Plan focuses on advancing capabilities in unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), system integration, and space technology, with aerospace engineers playing a pivotal role in leading innovation, product development, and advanced system design, particularly for space and defense applications. In the pharmaceutical and medical device sectors, the NIMP 2030 promotes the development of high-value products, including halal- certified medicines, biologics, and cutting-edge medical devices. Pharmaceutical and medical device engineers are essential to advancing R&D, ensuring compliance with international standards, and driving the creation of competitive, globally relevant technologies. 165 166

Stakeholders from the banking, machinery, and electronics sectors highlighted several challenges in recruiting electronics engineers. In the banking sector, electronic engineering skills are considered a niche, with few candidates possessing the specialised expertise required. The machinery and equipment sector faces similar issues, with many young engineers preferring to work with software rather than addressing plant operations directly. Additionally, many talents are drawn to semiconductor manufacturing due to its higher pay, making it difficult for the sector to compete with multinational corporations (MNCs) in terms of salaries. In the electrical and electronics (E&E) sector, candidates often lack the necessary skill sets to become engineers, and Gen-Z workers are increasingly focused on achieving a strong work-life balance, further complicating recruitment efforts. vi. Malaysia is already a global hub for E&E manufacturing, particularly in assembly, testing, and packaging. The semiconductor sector forms about 60% of total E&E exports, and the New Industrial Master Plan 2030 (NIMP 2030) aims to deepen Malaysia’s involvement in front-end activities like integrated circuit (IC) design, which has higher value-added potential. NIMP 2030 focuses on creating global IC design champions in areas such as electric vehicles (EV), renewable energy (RE), and artificial intelligence (AI). As Malaysia transitions from being an assembly hub to an innovation-driven economy, the need for IC design engineers becomes pivotal in driving these sectors. Wafer fabrication is another crucial element in semiconductor manufacturing. Malaysia aims to expand its capabilities beyond the current focus on packaging and testing. This step is essential to increase its control over the entire semiconductor value chain. The NIMP 2030 has set a strategy to attract global leaders in wafer fabrication and establish mid-tier wafer fabrication facilities domestically. Wafer fabrication engineers are needed to develop, operate, and maintain these facilities,

packaging and testing. This step is essential to increase its control over the entire semiconductor value chain. The Malaysian Institute of Accountants (MIA) highlighted the reasons for the current demand for accountants: • Compliance and Regulatory Changes: New accounting standards related to sustainability (S1 and S2) and revenue recognition (MFRS 15) are increasing the need for accountants to ensure compliance with reporting requirements set by regulatory bodies like Bursa Malaysia. Upcoming mandatory sustainability reporting in 2025 and 2026 further heightens this demand. • Technological Advancements and Digital Transformation: The rise of big data, cloud computing, and digital technologies is transforming the profession. Accountants must adapt by acquiring the latest technological skills to gather, interpret, and provide strategic insights, as well as integrate technology into areas like CPD programmes, reporting, and auditing. • Sustainability and ESG Reporting: The growing focus on sustainability and ESG has led to new reporting obligations. Accountants are crucial in helping organisations prepare for and comply with these standards, particularly in specialised areas like sustainability accounting. • Niche Industry Demands: Accountants

areas like CPD programmes, reporting, and auditing. • Sustainability and ESG Reporting: The growing focus on sustainability and ESG has led to new reporting obligations. Accountants are crucial in helping organisations prepare for and comply with these standards, particularly in specialised areas like sustainability accounting. • Niche Industry Demands: Accountants are in high demand across sectors like food manufacturing, aerospace, and construction due to complex accounting and reporting requirements. • Economic Growth and Shortage of Accountants: As Malaysia’s economy continues to expand, the demand for accountants is increasing. However, the current target of 60,000 accounting professionals has not been met, partly due to existing regulations that limit membership. New legislation is expected to address this shortage by introducing additional membership levels. • Expanding Roles and Specialisation: The evolving industry landscape is broadening accountants’ roles, necessitating specialised skills in areas like sustainability and green accounting to meet the diverse needs of businesses and organisations. 225 226

This occupation is included in the MyCOL. i. The job titles nominated reflect mostly the need for Cybersecurity Specialist, Information Security Officer, and Cybersecurity Analyst. ii. Stakeholders from the insurance and banking sectors highlighted a critical shortage of cybersecurity professionals. In the insurance sector, there is a global shortage of cybersecurity talent, with a particularly low supply of individuals possessing the specific technical skills, experience, and knowledge needed to protect against emerging threats. Similarly, the banking sector faces a significant lack of local candidates with the required expertise, making it difficult to meet the increasing demand for cybersecurity professionals in the country. iii. Based on input from the consultations, the rapid advancement of technology has created significant technical skills gaps among cybersecurity professionals in the insurance and banking sectors. As new areas of cybersecurity emerge, educational institutions often struggle to keep pace, leading to a shortage of professionals with the specialised skills needed to meet these evolving challenges. Key gaps include the lack of industry-specific qualifications, such as information security certifications, and insufficient working knowledge of the technical systems, products, and project or software delivery methodologies used within organisations. This underscores the need for targeted upskilling and continuous professional development. The level of experience most sought after for cybersecurity professionals in the insurance and banking sectors typically ranges from 3-4 years for mid-level roles, while more senior positions often require over 8 years of experience, reflecting the need for both foundational expertise and advanced technical skills in these fields. iv. To address the shortage of cybersecurity professionals, several measures have been implemented, including clear career development paths, key skills allowances, education assistance programmes, and attractive packages that include competitive salaries and benefits. These initiatives are designed to attract, retain, and upskill talent in the insurance and banking sectors. v. The

Stakeholders from the rubber-based products, electrical and electronics (E&E), automotive, and aluminium sectors highlighted challenges in recruiting electrical engineering technicians. Wafer fabrication is another crucial element in semiconductor manufacturing. Malaysia aims to expand its capabilities beyond the current focus on packaging and testing. This step is essential to increase its control over the entire semiconductor value chain. The NIMP 2030 has set a strategy to attract global leaders in wafer fabrication and establish mid-tier wafer fabrication facilities domestically. Wafer fabrication technicians are needed to develop, operate, and maintain these facilities, which are expected to drive Malaysia’s participation in global semiconductor production. This move aligns with Malaysia’s mission to diversify its industrial base and enter higher- value segments of manufacturing. 301 302

The Electrical and Electronics Association of Malaysia (TEEAM) highlighted the growing need for Electronics Engineering Technicians, particularly as industries advance towards greater automation. These technicians are essential for the installation and maintenance of electronic systems, which are becoming increasingly integral to modern manufacturing processes. TEEAM also highlighted a significant challenge in sourcing qualified Electronics Engineering Technicians due to the lack of candidates holding the necessary competency licenses. The limited availability of certification opportunities, with the government offering only two Certified Wireman Competency Examinations per year, restricts the number of qualified applicants. Additionally, private institutions face difficulties in meeting the compliance requirements for these certifications, further contributing to the shortage of skilled technicians. This limitation in intake exacerbates the already insufficient talent pool in the field. 305 306

Stakeholders from the O&G extraction support activities, timber, textiles, and automotive sectors highlighted several challenges in recruiting mechanical engineering technicians. In the O&G extraction support activities sector, there is a lack of awareness among Malaysian school leavers about the high-paying opportunities available in these fields, in contrast to countries like the USA, Europe, the Philippines, and Australia, where such skills are highly sought after. The timber sector faces challenges as candidates increasingly demand higher wages, driven by inflation and the weakening Ringgit. In the textiles industry, the location of many companies is unattractive to local candidates, and fresh graduates often lack the necessary skills, as educational institutions do not offer courses tailored to machinery and textiles. Similarly, the automotive sector struggles with a shortage of candidates who possess advanced technical skills, with many only having basic qualifications. 309 310

The The New Industrial Master Plan (NIMP 2030) emphasises the expansion of Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET) programmes to address skills mismatches by enhancing the quality of local talent for high-skilled jobs in critical sectors. In collaboration with the private sector, the strategy is to align TVET programmes with industry needs, offering flexible learning options, and providing career support services. It includes identifying critical high-skilled jobs, co-developing industry-relevant TVET syllabi, and building a strong brand for TVET programmes. It includes identifying critical high-skilled jobs, co-developing industry-relevant TVET syllabi, and building a strong brand for TVET programmes. The National TVET Council (MTVET), along with other government bodies, will lead this initiative, ensuring the programmes are tailored to meet the demands of critical sectors and offer job placement opportunities. The Higher Institution Centres of Excellence (HICoE) programme, an initiative by the Ministry of Higher Education (MOHE) is designed to elevate research excellence of Higher Education Institutions (HEIs). The programme seeks to identify and support high-performing Centres of Excellence (CoEs) that focus on specialised niche research areas. The Centre of Technology (COT) in Malaysian Polytechnics serves as a key reference hub, focusing on advancing TVET, promoting collaboration, research, and innovation. It aims to align educational programmes with national economic needs, producing highly skilled, entrepreneurial graduates, and building a strong institutional reputation. With a mission to excel through partnerships and continuous development, COT also seeks to diversify offerings to attract students and be recognised locally and internationally as a leader in its specialised fields. The 2u2i programme, initiated by MOHE, integrates two (2) years of university education with two (2) years of industry experience, enhances students' practical skills and aligns academic research with real-world industry needs, further strengthening the collaboration between academia and industry. This approach provides students with hands-on experience, better preparing them for the workforce while ensuring that their academic learning is relevant and adaptable to the evolving demands of the industry. The Academy in Industry (ADi) certifies individuals who are offered employment and can demonstrate competence as per the National Occupational Skills Standard (NOSS) standard. This is achieved through a combination of formal training, consisting of 20% theory and 80% work experience throughout the programme, after fulfilling the required NOSS standards. An initiative designed to create early career awareness

are offered employment and can demonstrate competence as per the National Occupational Skills Standard (NOSS) standard. This is achieved through a combination of formal training, consisting of 20% theory and 80% work experience throughout the programme, after fulfilling the required NOSS standards. An initiative designed to create early career awareness and foster skills development among secondary school students plays a crucial role in bridging the gap between education and the workforce. Such programmes, often launched in collaboration with education and labour ministries, aim to expose students to high-demand career pathways and job sectors. By incorporating career talks, profiling assessments, and workshops, these initiatives equip students with the knowledge to make informed decisions about their future education and career choices. In conclusion, promoting industry-academia collaboration in Malaysia is vital for closing the gap between education and industry needs, enhancing graduate employability and innovation. By combining established platforms like CREST, NIMP 2030, HICoE, 2u2i, and FSTC with the newly formed MyMahir-NAICI initiative, stakeholders have access to a robust ecosystem that both addresses immediate mismatches and guides long-term workforce planning tied to AI and emerging sectors. Fostering partnerships with global firms is a strategic approach to enhancing the experience and deepening the skills of the Malaysian workforce, while also making the country more attractive to top talent. It is part of strengthening the resilience and robustness of the supply chain. By collaborating with international companies, Malaysian firms can provide their employees with exposure to global best practices, cutting-edge technologies, and advanced training opportunities. These partnerships can lead to knowledge transfer, where local talent benefits from the expertise and innovations developed by global leaders in various industries. The Malaysian government has laid down policies that support such initiatives, including the National Investment Aspirations (NIA), which aim to attract quality investments that contribute to economic growth, job creation, and skills development. Through MIDA’s efforts and these supportive policies, Malaysia can ensure that its workforce remains competitive on a global scale by gaining exposure to international standards and practices. Two (2) examples, one recent and the other older, illustrate the importance of leveraging on this strategy. Intel Malaysia is a major player in the electronics and semiconductor industry. Since its establishment in 1972, Intel has invested over RM32 billion, with plans for an additional RM30 billion in the coming decade. The company focuses on fostering local talent through partnerships with Malaysian universities, offering scholarships, internships, and training programmes. Intel also drives technology transfer through Research and Development (R&D) initiatives, helping to build Malaysia’s capabilities in high-value-added manufacturing. Furthermore, Intel collaborates with educational institutions to align academic programmes with industry needs, particularly in STEM fields. It will provide Malaysian scientists, 1. https://myheart.my/ developers, and startups with access to cutting-edge AI computing resources. It is envisaged that AI-driven applications will create a variety of industries and transform existing ones, as is the experience of countries that are ahead of the curve. The investment will help Malaysia become a regional leader in AI, benefitting sectors such as Healthcare, Manufacturing, and Smart Cities. Talent development initiatives, e.g., training in AI technologies and infrastructure management will help grow local expertise. This initiative is aligned with Malaysia's ambitions to bolster its digital economy and develop a robust foundation for future AI advancements. By fostering these global partnerships and strengthening the supply chain, Malaysia can create a more dynamic and skilled workforce, ultimately leading to improved productivity and economic resilience. These efforts also contribute to retaining top talent, as employees are more likely to stay with companies that invest in their professional development and provide them with opportunities to grow on an international stage. This holistic approach not only improves workforce capabilities but also positions Malaysia competitively in the international market. 391 392

LAPORAN SURVEI TENAGA BURUH LABOUR FORCE SURVEY REPORT MALAYSIA SUKU TAHUN PERTAMA 2026 FIRST QUARTER 2026 Pemakluman Jabatan Perangkaan Malaysia (DOSM) sedang melaksanakan Banci Ekonomi 2026 (BE2026) dengan tema “Data Nadi Ekonomi Rakyat”. Pelaksanaan Banci Ekonomi kali keenam ini berlangsung dari 5 Januari hingga 31 Oktober 2026. BE2026 bertujuan untuk mengumpul data yang menyeluruh dan berstruktur daripada semua pertubuhan perniagaan berdaftar dan tidak berdaftar di Malaysia, bagi menilai prestasi, struktur serta ciri-ciri ekonomi negara secara komprehensif dan berasaskan bukti. Malaysia buat julung kalinya telah menduduki tangga pertama (1) di peringkat global dalam laporan dwi- tahunan Open Data Inventory (ODIN) 2024/25 yang dikeluarkan oleh Open Data Watch (ODW), mengatasi 197 negara lain. Pencapaian ini merupakan lonjakan ketara daripada kedudukan ke-67 dalam penilaian ODIN 2022/23. OpenDOSM NextGen adalah medium yang menyediakan katalog data dan visualisasi bagi memudahkan pengguna menganalisis pelbagai data dan boleh diakses melalui portal https://open.dosm.gov.my. Announcement The Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM) is conducting the Economic Census 2026 (BE2026), with themed “Data Nadi Ekonomi Rakyat”. The sixth Economic Census, will be carried out from 5th January to 31st October 2026. BE2026 aims to collect comprehensive and structured data from all registered and unregistered business establishments in Malaysia to assess the nation’s economic performance, structure and characteristics in an evidence-based manner. Malaysia has, for the first time, successfully secured the top position globally in the biennial Open Data Inventory (ODIN) 2024/25 report released by Open Data Watch (ODW), surpassing 197 other countries. This achievement marks a significant leap from its 67th position in the ODIN 2022/23 assessment. OpenDOSM NextGen is a medium that provides data catalogue and visualisations to facilitate users' analysis and can be accessed through https://open.dosm.gov.my. Nota Penerangan Bermula dengan penerbitan Laporan Survei Tenaga Buruh (STB), Suku Tahun Pertama 2026, Malaysia, statistik utama tenaga buruh dianggarkan berdasarkan anggaran penduduk semasa Malaysia. Seiring itu, data STB bagi Suku Tahun Pertama 2024 sehingga Suku Tahun Keempat 2025 turut disemak semula menggunakan data unjuran berdasarkan anggaran penduduk semasa terkini Sementara itu, semakan semula siri data STB Suku Tahun Kedua 2020 hingga Suku Tahun Keempat 2023 masih dilaksanakan. Data tersebut akan diterbitkan selepas proses analisis dan pemukatamadan data selesai dijalankan secara berperingkat. Bagi perincian penjadualan siri data semakan semula, sila layari https://newss. statistics.gov.my/. 8 LAPORAN SURVEI TENAGA BURUH | SUKU TAHUN PERTAMA 2026 LABOUR FORCE SURVEY REPORT | FIRST QUARTER 2026

Explanatory Notes Starting with the release of Labour Force Survey (LFS), First Quarter 2026, Malaysia, the principal statistics of the labour force are estimated based on the current population estimates of Malaysia. On the same note, revisions are carried out on LFS data for the First Quarter of 2024 to the Fourth Quarter of 2025 using projection data based on the latest current population estimates. Meanwhile, the review of the LFS data series for the Second Quarter of 2020 to the Fourth Quarter of 2023 is still ongoing. The data will be published progressively upon the completion of the analysis and finalisation process. For the details on tabulation of revised data series, please visit https://newss. statistics.gov.my/. 1. Pengenalan Ekonomi Malaysia pada suku tahun pertama 2026 mencatat pertumbuhan Keluaran Dalam Negeri Kasar (KDNK) awalan sebanyak 5.3 peratus, berbanding 6.3 peratus pada suku tahun sebelumnya. Peningkatan ini didorong oleh prestasi yang stabil dalam sektor Perkhidmatan, Pembuatan, Pembinaan dan Pertanian, manakala sektor Perlombongan & pengkuarian mencatatkan penurunan pada suku tahun ini. Dari segi prestasi suku tahun ke suku tahun, ekonomi negara menguncup sebanyak 4.4 peratus berbanding pertumbuhan positif pada suku tahun keempat 2025 sebanyak 3.3 peratus. Memasuki tahun 2026, ekonomi Malaysia menunjukkan tumpuan yang jelas terhadap pengukuhan kesejahteraan rakyat dan rangsangan permintaan domestik melalui pelaksanaan Sumbangan Asas Rahmah (SARA) dan Sumbangan Tunai Rahmah (STR). Kerajaan memperkenalkan SARA Bulanan dengan peruntukan sekitar RM8 bilion kepada 8.1 juta penerima serta bantuan one-off “SARA untuk Semua” kepada kira-kira 22 juta rakyat dewasa, mencerminkan dasar fiskal yang lebih ekspansif dan inklusif dalam menangani kos sara hidup1 Inisiatif ini bukan sahaja meningkatkan kuasa beli rakyat, malah merangsang perbelanjaan pengguna khususnya menjelang musim perayaan, di samping memperkukuh sektor runcit melalui penglibatan lebih banyak premis tempatan. Kadar penggunaan yang tinggi turut menunjukkan keberkesanan penyasaran dasar dalam menyokong permintaan agregat domestik, sekali gus menyumbang kepada kestabilan dan pertumbuhan ekonomi negara secara inklusif. Peningkatan aktiviti ekonomi sempena musim perayaan dan pertumbuhan ekonomi digital terus memperlihatkan impak yang ketara terhadap dinamik pasaran buruh yang dinamik di Malaysia. Penganjuran jualan perayaan dan jualan tengah malam di pusat premium outlets telah merangsang perbelanjaan pengguna serta meningkatkan permintaan tenaga kerja, khususnya dalam sektor runcit dan perkhidmatan2. Selain itu, peningkatan kunjungan ke bazar Aidilfitri seperti di Jalan Tuanku Abdul Rahman mencerminkan pengukuhan aktiviti ekonomi domestik yang turut menyokong pendapatan peniaga kecil dan pekerja tidak formal3. Dalam masa yang sama, peranan platform digital semakin signifikan apabila TikTok menyumbang sekitar RM20 bilion kepada ekonomi digital negara serta menyokong kira-kira 147,000 peluang pekerjaan, terutamanya dalam kalangan

Aidilfitri seperti di Jalan Tuanku Abdul Rahman mencerminkan pengukuhan aktiviti ekonomi domestik yang turut menyokong pendapatan peniaga kecil dan pekerja tidak formal3. Dalam masa yang sama, peranan platform digital semakin signifikan apabila TikTok menyumbang sekitar RM20 bilion kepada ekonomi digital negara serta menyokong kira-kira 147,000 peluang pekerjaan, terutamanya dalam kalangan perusahaan mikro, kecil dan sederhana (PMKS)4. Secara analitikal, situasi ini menunjukkan bahawa permintaan bermusim dan pendigitalan ekonomi saling melengkapi dalam merangsang perwujudan jawatan, meningkatkan kadar penyertaan tenaga buruh serta memperluas sumber pendapatan, sekali gus menyokong pertumbuhan ekonomi yang lebih mampan dan inklusif. Tambahan pula, perkembangan pelaburan strategik, reformasi pasaran buruh dan perlindungan sosial pekerja terus memperkukuh struktur ekonomi dan tenaga buruh yang dinamik di Malaysia. Pelaburan bernilai RM30 bilion oleh Intel dalam fasiliti pembungkusan semikonduktor termaju dijangka menjadi pemangkin kepada pembangunan industri bernilai tinggi, sekali gus mewujudkan peluang pekerjaan berkemahiran tinggi serta meningkatkan keupayaan teknologi negara5. Dalam masa yang sama, keperluan memperkukuh hubungan antara gaji dan produktiviti terus diberi penekanan bagi memastikan pertumbuhan pendapatan pekerja selari dengan peningkatan kecekapan dan kemahiran tenaga buruh6. Konsep dan definisi Pelaksanaan Survei Tenaga Buruh di Malaysia adalah berdasarkan garis panduan dan syor International Labour Organization (ILO) dengan merujuk Surveys of Economically Active Population, Employment, Unemployment and Underemployment: An ILO Manual on Concepts and Methods. Paparan 1 : Konsep dan Definisi PENDUDUK UMUR BEKERJA Semua orang yang berumur 15 hingga 64 tahun, sama ada berada di dalam tenaga buruh atau di luar tenaga buruh. TENAGA BURUH Semua orang yang berada dalam kumpulan umur bekerja, sama ada bekerja atau menganggur. PENDUDUK BEKERJA Semua orang yang bekerja sekurang-kurangnya sejam semasa minggu rujukan untuk mendapatkan upah, keuntungan atau keuntungan keluarga sama ada sebagai majikan, pekerja, bekerja sendiri atau pekerja keluarga tanpa gaji. PENGANGGUR Semua orang yang tidak bekerja semasa minggu rujukan dan dikelaskan kepada dua kumpulan iaitu penganggur aktif dan penganggur tidak aktif. LUAR TENAGA BURUH Semua orang yang tidak dikelaskan sebagai bekerja atau menganggur dikategorikan sebagai luar tenaga buruh. Kategori ni terdiri daripada suri rumah, pelajar (termasuk mereka yang akan melanjutkan pelajaran), mereka yang telah bersara, tidak berkeupayaan dan tidak berminat untuk mencari pekerjaan. 3. Kadar Penyertaan Tenaga Buruh (KPTB) kekal stabil pada 70.9 peratus bagi suku tahun pertama 2026, mengekalkan kadar yang sama seperti yang dicatatkan pada suku tahun sebelumnya. Penduduk bekerja Bilangan penduduk bekerja terus meningkat dengan merekodkan pertumbuhan sebanyak 0.3 peratus Bilangan penduduk bekerja terus mencatatkan pertumbuhan positif pada suku tahun pertama 2026, meningkat sebanyak 0.3 peratus (+48.5 ribu orang) kepada 16.73 juta orang (ST4 2025r: 16.68 juta orang). Dari segi tahun ke tahun, jumlah penduduk bekerja meningkat sebanyak 1.0 peratus, atau bersamaan peningkatan 161.5 ribu orang berbanding suku tahun pertama 2025 (ST1 2025r: 16.56 juta orang). Lelaki merangkumi 62.6 peratus daripada jumlah guna tenaga dengan 10.47 juta orang, meningkat sebanyak 0.2 peratus berbanding suku tahun sebelumnya. Sementara itu, wanita mewakili 37.4 peratus daripada jumlah guna tenaga, meningkat 0.4 peratus untuk merekodkan 6.25 juta orang. Dari segi kumpulan umur, penduduk bekerja kebanyakannya dalam kumpulan umur 25 hingga 34 tahun, iaitu merangkumi 30.6 peratus (5.11 juta orang). Kumpulan ini diikuti oleh mereka yang berumur 35 hingga 44 tahun pada 27.5 peratus (4.61 juta orang) dan kumpulan umur 45 hingga 54 tahun sebanyak 18.1 peratus (3.02 juta orang). Merujuk kepada pencapaian pendidikan, penduduk bekerja berpendidikan menengah mewakili 56.6 peratus daripada jumlah penduduk bekerja dengan 9.47 juta orang (ST4 2025r: 56.6%; 9.45 juta orang). Sementara itu, penduduk bekerja berpendidikan tertiari merupakan kategori kedua terbesar sebanyak 32.8 peratus atau 5.49 juta orang (ST4 2025r: 32.8%; 5.47 juta orang), seperti yang ditunjukkan dalam Paparan 5. Pemahaman yang komprehensif berkenaan pasaran buruh memerlukan analisis indikator tambahan di samping indikator pengangguran. Oleh itu, indikator bekerja kurang daripada 30 jam seminggu, guna tenaga tidak penuh berkaitan masa dan guna tenaga tidak penuh berkaitan kemahiran dapat memberi lebih banyak gambaran mengenai kedudukan penawaran tenaga buruh Malaysia selaras dengan situasi semasa. Pekerja yang bekerja kurang daripada 30 jam seminggu merujuk kepada individu yang bekerja kurang daripada 30 jam dalam minggu rujukan disebabkan keadaan kerja atau kerana kerja tidak mencukupi. Pada suku tahun pertama 2026, bilangan pekerja dalam kategori ini meningkat sebanyak 2.5 peratus kepada 231.6 ribu orang (ST4 2025r: 226.0 ribu orang). Sementara itu, kadar penduduk bekerja kurang daripada 30 jam seminggu kekal pada 1.4 peratus pada suku tahun pertama 2026 (ST4 2025r: 1.4%), seperti yang digambarkan dalam Carta 5. Pengangguran dan kadar pengangguran Kadar pengangguran menurun sebanyak 0.1 mata peratus kepada 2.9 peratus Bilangan penganggur menyusut sedikit sebanyak 0.4 peratus pada suku tahun pertama 2026, mencecah 506.5 ribu orang berbanding 508.3 ribu orang pada suku tahun sebelumnya. Sementara itu, kadar pengangguran menurun sebanyak 0.1 mata peratus kepada 2.9 peratus. Perbandingan tahun ke tahun menunjukkan bilangan penganggur berkurang sebanyak 3.1 peratus (-16.1 ribu orang) berbanding suku tahun pertama 2025 (Q1 2025r: 29.0 ribu orang), yang membawa kepada kadar pengangguran sebanyak 2.9 peratus, berbanding 3.1 peratus yang dicatatkan pada tahun sebelumnya. Mengikut kategori pengangguran, penganggur aktif atau mereka yang bersedia untuk bekerja dan aktif mencari pekerjaan merangkumi 79.8 peratus daripada jumlah keseluruhan penganggur. 1. Introduction Malaysia’s economy in the first quarter of 2026, recorded an advance Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth of 5.3 per cent, compared to 6.3 per cent in the preceding quarter. This growth was driven by stable performance in the Services, Manufacturing, Construction, and Agriculture sectors, while the Mining and Quarrying sector recorded a decline during the quarter. On a quarter-on-quarter basis, the national economy contracted significantly by 4.4 per cent, compared to a positive growth of 3.3 per cent in the fourth quarter of 2025. Entering 2026, Malaysia’s economy demonstrates a clear focus on strengthening public well-being and stimulating domestic demand through the implementation of Sumbangan Asas Rahmah (SARA) and Sumbangan Tunai Rahmah (STR). The Government introduced the monthly SARA programme with an allocation of approximately RM8 billion benefiting 8.1 million recipients, alongside a one-off “SARA untuk Semua” assistance of RM100 for about 22 million adult Malaysians, reflecting a more expansionary and inclusive fiscal policy in addressing the cost of living1. These initiatives not only enhance households’ purchasing power but also stimulate consumer spending, particularly ahead of festive seasons, while reinforcing the retail sector through greater participation of local outlets. The high utilisation rate of these assistance programmes further indicates effective policy targeting in supporting domestic aggregate demand, thereby contributing to overall economic stability and inclusive growth. The increase in economic activities during festive seasons, alongside the continued expansion of the digital economy, has demonstrated a significant impact on the dynamics of Malaysia’s labour market. The organisation of festive sales and midnight sales at premium outlet centres has stimulated consumer spending and increased labour demand, particularly in the retail and services sectors2. Furthermore, the rise in visitor traffic to Aidilfitri bazaars, such as those in Jalan Tuanku Abdul Rahman, reflects the strengthening of domestic economic activity, which also supports

sales and midnight sales at premium outlet centres has stimulated consumer spending and increased labour demand, particularly in the retail and services sectors2. Furthermore, the rise in visitor traffic to Aidilfitri bazaars, such as those in Jalan Tuanku Abdul Rahman, reflects the strengthening of domestic economic activity, which also supports the income of small traders and informal workers3. At the same time, the role of digital platforms has become increasingly significant, with TikTok contributing approximately RM20 billion to the national digital economy and supporting around 147,000 job opportunities, particularly among micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs)4. From an analytical perspective, this situation indicates that seasonal demand and economic digitalisation complement each other in driving job creation, increasing labour force participation, and diversifying income sources, thereby supporting more sustainable and inclusive economic growth. Notably, the advancement of strategic investments, labour market reforms, and worker social protection continues to strengthen Malaysia’s economic structure and labour market dynamics. The RM30 billion investment by Intel in an advanced semiconductor packaging facility is expected to serve as a catalyst for the development of high-value industries, while creating high skilled employment opportunities and enhancing the nation’s technological capabilities5. At the same time, greater emphasis is being placed on strengthening the linkage between wages and productivity to ensure that income growth is aligned with improvements in labour efficiency and skills6. In addition, the proposed implementation of mandatory contributions for gig workers is viewed as an important step in expanding social protection and safeguarding the long-term economic wellbeing of this group7. The Labour Force Survey Report for the first quarter of 2026 presents statistics of the labour force based on the Labour Force Survey conducted by the Department of Statistics Malaysia. The survey is carried out using probability sampling through a household approach to collect data on the structure of the labour force, employment, and unemployment, covering Malaysian citizens and non-citizens. The principal statistics are presented according to selected demographic and socioeconomic characteristics such as sex, age group, educational attainment, status in employment and occupation. The report elaborates on the quarter on-quarter changes to examine the immediate effect of socioeconomic events that occurred throughout the quarter. Users are advised to interpret these short-term changes with caution since the data are not seasonally adjusted. In addition, annual changes from the same quarter of the preceding year are also reported. 2. Concepts and definitions The implementation of Labour Force Survey in Malaysia is based on the guidelines and recommendations of the International Labour Organization (ILO) with reference to the Surveys of Economically Active Population, Employment, Unemployment and Underemployment: An ILO Manual on Concepts and Methods. Exhibit 1: Concepts and Definitions WORKING AGE POPULATION All persons aged between 15 to 64 years who are either in the labour force or outside the labour force. LABOUR FORCE All persons in the working age who are either employed or unemployed. EMPLOYED All persons who at any time during the reference week worked at least one hour for pay, profit or family gain either as employers, employees, own-account workers or unpaid family workers. UNEMPLOYED All persons who did not work during the reference week and are classified into

employed or unemployed. EMPLOYED All persons who at any time during the reference week worked at least one hour for pay, profit or family gain either as employers, employees, own-account workers or unpaid family workers. UNEMPLOYED All persons who did not work during the reference week and are classified into two groups that are actively unemployed and inactively unemployed. OUTSIDE LABOUR FORCE All persons not classified as employed or unemployed are classified as outside labour force. This category consists of housewives, students (including those going for further studies), retired, disabled person and those not interested in looking for jobs. 28 LAPORAN SURVEI TENAGA BURUH | SUKU TAHUN PERTAMA 2026 LABOUR FORCE SURVEY REPORT | FIRST QUARTER 2026

3. Labour force The labour force participation rate remained unchanged at 70.9 per cent The labour force expanded by 0.3 per cent in the first quarter of 2026, adding 46.6 thousand persons to reach a total of 17.23 million persons (Q4 2025r: 17.19 million persons). The Labour Force Participation Rate (LFPR) stood at 70.9 per cent in the first quarter of 2026, maintaining the same level observed in the preceding quarter. The employment-to-population ratio, a key measure of an economy's capacity for job creation, held steady at 68.8 per cent. During the same period, the number of outside labour force grew by 17.5 thousand persons, reaching a total of 7.08 million persons (Q4 2025r: 7.06 million persons) as shown in Exhibit 2. Compared to the first quarter of the previous year, the labour force rose by 145.4 thousand persons (Q1 2025r: 17.09 million persons). Additionally, the LFPR saw an increase of 0.1 percentage points (Q1 2025r: 70.8%). Putrajaya recorded the highest labour force participation rate at 79.2 per cent At the national level, the LFPR remained unchanged at 70.9 per cent in the first quarter of 2026. 4. Employed persons The number of employed persons continued to expand, recording a growth of 0.3 per cent The number of employed persons continued to register a positive growth in the first quarter of 2026, increasing by 0.3 per cent (+48.5 thousand persons) to 16.73 million persons (Q4 2025r: 16.68 million persons). On a year-on-year comparison, total employment edged up by 1.0 per cent, or equivalent to an increase of 161.5 thousand persons compared to the first quarter of 2025 (Q1 2025r: 16.56 million persons). Males comprised 62.6 per cent of the employed persons with 10.47 million persons, rose up by 0.2 per cent compared to the previous quarter. Meanwhile, females represented 37.4 per cent of the employed persons, an increase of 0.4 per cent to record 6.25 million persons. In terms of age group, the majority of employed persons were in the 25 to 34 years age group, accounting for 30.6 per cent (5.11 million persons). This was followed by those aged 35 to 44 at 27.5 per cent (4.61 million persons), and those aged 45 to 54 at 18.1 per cent (3.02 million persons). According to educational attainment, employed persons with secondary education represented 56.6 per cent of total employment with 9.47 million persons (Q4 2025r: 56.6%; 9.45 million persons). Meanwhile, employed persons with tertiary education were the second-largest category at 32.8 per cent or 5.49 million persons (Q4 2025r: 32.8%; 5.47 million persons), as shown in Exhibit 5. Unemployed and unemployment rate Unemployment rate dropped by 0.1 percentage points to 2.9 per cent The number of unemployed persons edged down by 0.4 per cent during the first quarter of 2026, reaching 506.5 thousand compared to 508.3 thousand persons in the previous quarter. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate decreased by 0.1 percentage point to 2.9 per cent. Year-on-year comparison showed the number of unemployed persons fell by 3.1 per cent (-16.1 thousand persons) compared to first quarter of 2025 (Q1 2025r: -29.0 thousand persons), leading to a 2.9 per cent unemployment rate, an improvement from the 3.1 per cent seen in the previous year. By category of unemployment, the actively unemployed or those who were available for work and actively seeking jobs consists of 79.8 per cent of the total unemployed persons. This group decreased by 0.2 per cent recording 404.0 thousand persons during the first quarter of 2026, compared to 405.0 thousand persons in the fourth quarter of 2025, (Q4 2025r: 79.7%). Meanwhile, the inactively unemployed or those who believed that there were no jobs available, decreased by 0.8 thousand persons to 102.5 thousand persons (Q4 2025r: 103.4 thousand

404.0 thousand persons during the first quarter of 2026, compared to 405.0 thousand persons in the fourth quarter of 2025, (Q4 2025r: 79.7%). 1. Pengenalan 1.1 Statistik yang diterbitkan dalam laporan ini adalah berdasarkan Survei Tenaga Buruh (STB) yang dilaksanakan oleh Jabatan Perangkaan Malaysia (DOSM). STB dilaksanakan berdasarkan garis panduan dan syor International Labour Organization (ILO) dengan merujuk kepada Surveys of Economically Active Population, Employment, Unemployment and Underemployment: An ILO Manual on Concepts and Methods. 1.2 Survei yang dijalankan setiap bulan melalui pendekatan isi rumah ini menyediakan statistik tenaga buruh, guna tenaga dan pengangguran di peringkat nasional dan negeri serta kawasan bandar dan luar bandar. 1.3 Pengumpulan dan prosesan data mengekalkan pendekatan yang menyeluruh dan sistematik dari semasa ke semasa untuk mendapatkan perbandingan statistik siri masa. 1.4 Maklumat yang dikumpul melalui STB adalah mengikut peruntukan Akta Perangkaan 1965 (Disemak 1989). Seksyen 6 dibawah Akta ini mengkehendaki mana-mana individu perlu memberikan butiran maklumat sebenar atau anggaran terbaik kepada DOSM. Sementara itu, Seksyen 7 dalam Akta yang sama memperuntukan denda kepada responden yang gagal memberi kerjasama dalam perlaksanaan penyiasatan oleh DOSM. Mengikut Akta ini, maklumat terperinci yang dikumpul adalah sulit dan hanya angka agregat diterbitkan. 1.5 Nota teknikal ini menyediakan penerangan terperinci mengenai konsep, definisi dan metodologi yang digunakan dalam pelaksanaan STB bagi membantu pengguna untuk memahami dengan lebih mendalam berkaitan maklumat pasaran buruh yang dipungut melalui pendekatan isi rumah. 2. Objektif survei 2.1 Objektif utama STB ialah: i. Mengumpul maklumat berkaitan struktur dan taburan tenaga buruh, guna tenaga dan pengangguran; dan ii. Menyediakan statistik terkini berkaitan situasi pasaran buruh dari aspek penawaran tenaga buruh 2.2 Statistik daripada survei ini digunakan terutamanya oleh kerajaan sebagai input dalam merangka strategi pembangunan negara; begitu juga ahli ekonomi, ahli akademik, sektor swasta dan individu sebagai tujuan penyelidikan dan analisis. 3. Kaedah pengumpulan data 3.1 STB menggunakan kaedah temu ramah bersemuka dengan responden. Dalam tempoh survei, penemuramah terlatih melawat isi rumah di tempat kediaman (TK) yang terpilih untuk memperolehi

input dalam merangka strategi pembangunan negara; begitu juga ahli ekonomi, ahli akademik, sektor swasta dan individu sebagai tujuan penyelidikan dan analisis. 3. Kaedah pengumpulan data 3.1 STB menggunakan kaedah temu ramah bersemuka dengan responden. Dalam tempoh survei, penemuramah terlatih melawat isi rumah di tempat kediaman (TK) yang terpilih untuk memperolehi maklumat demografi semua ahli isi rumah dan maklumat terperinci berkaitan butir-butir tenaga buruh bagi ahli isi rumah yang berumur 15 tahun dan lebih. 106 LAPORAN SURVEI TENAGA BURUH | SUKU TAHUN PERTAMA 2026 LAPORAN SURVEI TENAGA BURUH | SUKU TAHUN PERTAMA 2026 107 LABOUR FORCE SURVEY REPORT | FIRST QUARTER 2026 LABOUR FORCE SURVEY REPORT | FIRST QUARTER 2026

3.2 Semakan kerja luar dibuat untuk mengesan dan memperbaiki kemungkinan maklumat yang salah atau tertinggal semasa penyiasatan ini dijalankan. Di samping itu, isi rumah terpilih juga ditemuramah semula untuk menyemak kualiti operasi pungutan data. 3.3 Sebanyak 25 peratus daripada panel ulangan terpilih ditemuramah menggunakan kaedah Computer-Assisted Telephone Interviewing (CATI)1. 4. Skop dan liputan 4.1 STB meliputi kedua-dua kawasan bandar dan luar bandar bagi daerah pentadbiran dalam semua negeri di Malaysia. 4.2 Definisi populasi survei meliputi penduduk yang tinggal di TK persendirian dan tidak termasuk mereka yang tinggal di TK institusi seperti hotel, asrama, hospital, penjara, rumah tumpangan dan pekerja yang tinggal di rumah kongsi. 4.3 Tiada penyelarasan dilakukan kepada penduduk yang tinggal di TK institusi untuk diasingkan daripada anggaran penduduk pertengahan tahun selepas banci walaupun STB tidak meliputi penduduk tersebut. Berdasarkan Banci Penduduk dan Perumahan semasa, penduduk yang tinggal di TK institusi adalah kurang daripada empat peratus daripada jumlah penduduk. Peratusan ini adalah kecil dan tidak mempengaruhi anggaran statistik tenaga buruh. 4.4 STB merangkumi penduduk yang aktif dan tidak aktif dalam ekonomi yang berumur 15 hingga 64 tahun2. Penduduk yang aktif dalam ekonomi terdiri daripada mereka yang bekerja atau menganggur, manakala mereka yang tidak aktif dikelaskan sebagai di luar tenaga buruh. 5. Rangka dan pensampelan 5.1 Rangka yang digunakan bagi pemilihan sampel STB Suku Tahun Pertama 2026 adalah berdasarkan Rangka Pensampelan Isi Rumah yang terdiri daripada blok penghitungan (BP) yang diwujudkan untuk pelaksanaan Banci Penduduk dan Perumahan 2020 dan dikemas kini dari semasa ke semasa. 5.2 BP merupakan suatu kawasan muka bumi yang diwujudkan untuk tujuan pelaksanaan operasi pungutan data yang secara puratanya mengandungi antara 80 hingga 120 TK. Semua BP dibentuk dalam lingkungan sempadan yang diwartakan iaitu di dalam mukim atau kawasan majlis tempatan. 5.3 BP dalam rangka pensampelan dikelaskan mengikut kawasan bandar dan luar bandar3. i. Kawasan bandar Kawasan yang diwartakan serta kawasan tepu bina yang bersempadan dengannya dan gabungan kedua-dua kawasan ini mempunyai penduduk seramai 10,000 orang atau lebih semasa Banci Penduduk dan Perumahan 2020. 1.
